title: Pea & Lentil Sprout Bowl
description: Freshly sprouted peas and lentils are among the richest dietary sources of Diamine Oxidase (DAO), the enzyme that breaks down histamine in the gut. This bowl is your first line of defense.
ingredients: 1 cup fresh pea sprouts (2–3 days old)
½ cup lentil sprouts (green or brown)
½ cup cooked, cooled white rice
method: Rinse sprouts thoroughly in cold water and pat dry.
Arrange sprouts over cooled rice — heat degrades DAO enzyme activity.
Drizzle hemp seed oil (rich in anti-inflammatory omega-3/6 ratio).
Add microplaned ginger — a natural mast cell stabilizer.
Season with pink salt and serve immediately.

functionalMarkers.label: DAO Enzyme Support
functionalMarkers.detail: Pea & lentil sprouts are among the highest food sources of DAO, directly supporting histamine degradation in the gut lumen.

functionalMarkers.label: Mast Cell Stabilization
functionalMarkers.detail: Ginger (gingerols) inhibits mast cell degranulation and reduces prostaglandin synthesis.

functionalMarkers.label: Omega Balance
functionalMarkers.detail: Hemp seed oil provides an optimal 3:1 omega-6:omega-3 ratio, reducing systemic inflammatory signaling.

functionalMarkers.label: Low Histamine Load
functionalMarkers.detail: All ingredients are fresh and unfermented — critical for MCAS/histamine intolerance.

avoidNote: Do not use canned lentils — fermentation/aging dramatically increases histamine content. Always use fresh sprouts the same day.

title: Broccoli & Quercetin Soup
description: Broccoli activates NRF2 pathways via sulforaphane, reducing oxidative stress and inflammatory cytokines. Combined with quercetin-rich onion and apple, this soup is a mast cell stabilizer in a bowl.
ingredients: 3 cups filtered water or low-histamine bone-free broth
¼ tsp black pepper (enhances curcumin absorption 2000%)
method: Sauté onion and garlic in olive oil on low heat for 5 minutes.
Add broccoli, apple, turmeric, and pepper. Stir to coat.
Pour in water/broth and bring to a gentle simmer for 12 minutes.
Blend until smooth. Do not overcook — sulforaphane is heat-sensitive.
Serve warm (not hot) with fresh parsley on top.

functionalMarkers.label: NRF2 / Sulforaphane
functionalMarkers.detail: Broccoli sulforaphane activates NRF2, the master regulator of antioxidant response, reducing IL-6, TNF-α and histamine receptor upregulation.

functionalMarkers.label: Quercetin (Natural Antihistamine)
functionalMarkers.detail: Onion is exceptionally high in quercetin, a flavonoid that inhibits mast cell degranulation and histamine release — acts like a natural antihistamine.

functionalMarkers.label: Curcumin + Piperine
functionalMarkers.detail: Turmeric + black pepper reduces NF-κB activation. Piperine increases curcumin bioavailability by ~2000%.

functionalMarkers.label: Inflammatory Cytokines
functionalMarkers.detail: This combination targets IL-4, IL-13 reduction — both implicated in MCAS-driven hypersensitivity.

avoidNote: Skip black pepper if salicylate sensitivity is a concern. Use white pepper as an alternative.

title: Anti-Migraine Magnesium Seed Butter
description: Magnesium deficiency is found in over 50% of migraine patients. Pumpkin seeds are the richest food source of magnesium. This seed butter replaces nut butters (high histamine) with a functional, calming spread.
ingredients: 1 tbsp MCT oil or coconut oil
½ tsp vanilla powder (not extract — contains alcohol)
method: Toast seeds lightly at 150°C/300°F for 8 min — do not brown.
Cool completely before blending — important for histamine control.
Blend seeds in a high-speed blender until a paste forms.
Add MCT oil gradually, vanilla, honey and salt.
Blend until smooth and creamy. Store in glass jar, refrigerate up to 5 days.

functionalMarkers.label: Magnesium (Migraine)
functionalMarkers.detail: Pumpkin seeds provide ~156mg magnesium per 30g serving. Magnesium blocks NMDA receptor activation — key in cortical spreading depression (migraine aura).

functionalMarkers.label: Zinc (Mast Cell)
functionalMarkers.detail: Zinc deficiency is associated with increased mast cell reactivity. Pumpkin seeds are the highest food source of zinc after oysters.

functionalMarkers.label: Tryptophan → Serotonin
functionalMarkers.detail: Sunflower seeds are rich in tryptophan, precursor to serotonin — low serotonin is a key migraine trigger.

functionalMarkers.label: HAT / Alpha Tryptase
functionalMarkers.detail: For Hereditary Alpha Tryptasemia patients: magnesium helps regulate tryptase-driven mast cell hyperreactivity.

avoidNote: Avoid almond, peanut, cashew, and walnut butters — all are high in histamine or histamine-liberating compounds.

title: Golden Millet Porridge
description: Millet is a gluten-free ancient grain that is exceptionally low histamine, soothing to inflamed gut mucosa, and acts as a prebiotic without feeding dysbiotic bacteria that produce histamine.
ingredients: 1 tbsp coconut oil or ghee (if tolerated)
method: Rinse millet thoroughly and dry-toast in pan for 2 minutes.
Add water, turmeric, cinnamon. Bring to boil then reduce to simmer.
Cover and cook 15 minutes until water absorbed and millet fluffy.
Stir in coconut oil or ghee off heat.
Top with pumpkin seed butter, fresh blueberries and a drizzle of honey.

functionalMarkers.label: Gut Mucosa Repair
functionalMarkers.detail: Millet is rich in ferulic acid and beta-glucans that coat and repair inflamed gut epithelium — critical since 70% of mast cells reside in the GI tract.

functionalMarkers.label: Prebiotic Fiber
functionalMarkers.detail: Millet supports Lactobacillus growth without feeding histamine-producing bacteria like Lactobacillus casei or helveticus.

functionalMarkers.label: Anthocyanins
functionalMarkers.detail: Fresh blueberries provide anthocyanins that inhibit mast cell IgE-mediated degranulation and reduce IL-4 levels.

functionalMarkers.label: Blood Sugar Stability
functionalMarkers.detail: Low glycemic index — blood sugar spikes can trigger cortisol release, which destabilizes mast cells.

avoidNote: Avoid dried fruits (high histamine), maple syrup (sulfites), or yogurt toppings — all are problematic for MCAS.

title: Zucchini Noodles with Basil Drizzle
description: A pasta replacement that avoids nightshade tomatoes entirely. Fresh basil contains rosmarinic acid — a potent mast cell stabilizer. Light, satisfying, and deeply anti-inflammatory.
ingredients: Juice of ½ fresh lemon (use sparingly)
method: Make basil drizzle: blend basil, olive oil, garlic, hemp seeds, salt until smooth.
Spiralize zucchini into noodles. Pat dry with paper towel.
Optional: sauté zucchini noodles in olive oil for 2 minutes only — keep al dente.
Toss warm noodles with basil drizzle.
Top with hemp seeds, nutritional yeast, and a small squeeze of lemon.

functionalMarkers.label: Rosmarinic Acid
functionalMarkers.detail: Basil is exceptionally rich in rosmarinic acid, which inhibits mast cell degranulation and blocks prostaglandin E2 synthesis.

functionalMarkers.label: COX-2 Inhibition
functionalMarkers.detail: Olive oil's oleocanthal acts as a natural COX-2 inhibitor — same mechanism as ibuprofen, without GI mast cell irritation.

functionalMarkers.label: Nightshade-Free
functionalMarkers.detail: Replaces tomato-based sauces which are high in histamine and contain solanine — a mast cell and nerve sensitizer.

functionalMarkers.label: Silicon & Collagen Co-factors
functionalMarkers.detail: Zucchini is rich in silicon, supporting gut lining collagen synthesis — important for leaky gut which drives MCAS flares.

avoidNote: Skip lemon if highly reactive. Nutritional yeast contains glutamates — test in small amounts. Parmesan is high histamine — avoid.

title: NRF2-Boosting Broccoli Sprout Bowl for MCAS & Histamine Intolerance
description: This powerhouse bowl combines sulforaphane-rich broccoli sprouts with quercetin-loaded onions and turmeric to activate NRF2 pathways and reduce systemic inflammation. Fresh pea sprouts provide natural DAO enzyme support while rosmarinic acid from fresh herbs stabilizes mast cells for comprehensive MCAS management.
metaDescription: Anti-inflammatory NRF2-boosting bowl with broccoli sprouts, quercetin-rich onions & fresh herbs. Perfect for MCAS, histamine intolerance & reducing inflammation.

longTailKeywords: what foods activate NRF2 for histamine intolerance
how to make sulforaphane rich meals for MCAS
best anti-inflammatory recipes for mast cell activation

schema.keywords: NRF2 activation, sulforaphane, broccoli sprouts, MCAS, histamine intolerance, anti-inflammatory, quercetin, mast cell stabilizing

method: Heat 2 tablespoons olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Add sliced onions and cook for 5-6 minutes until softened and translucent.
Add broccoli florets to the pan and cook for 4 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add sugar snap peas, turmeric, black pepper, and salt. Cook for 2-3 minutes until peas are bright green and tender-crisp.
Remove from heat and let cool for 2 minutes to preserve heat-sensitive compounds.
In a large serving bowl, combine the cooked vegetables with fresh broccoli sprouts and pea sprouts.
Whisk together remaining olive oil, lemon juice, and chopped fresh herbs to create a dressing.
Pour dressing over the bowl and toss gently to combine.
Top with pumpkin seeds and serve immediately while warm.

functionalMarkers.label: Sulforaphane Activation
functionalMarkers.detail: Broccoli sprouts contain 50-100x more sulforaphane than mature broccoli, activating NRF2 antioxidant pathways and reducing inflammation

functionalMarkers.label: Quercetin Mast Cell Support
functionalMarkers.detail: Onions provide natural quercetin which stabilizes mast cell degranulation and reduces histamine release

functionalMarkers.label: DAO Enzyme Boost
functionalMarkers.detail: Fresh pea sprouts naturally contain diamine oxidase enzyme precursors supporting histamine breakdown

functionalMarkers.label: Rosmarinic Acid
functionalMarkers.detail: Fresh basil and oregano provide rosmarinic acid, a potent mast cell stabilizer with anti-inflammatory properties

avoidNote: Consume immediately after preparation. Sulforaphane and DAO enzymes degrade rapidly - do not store as leftovers.
